name: pspy

usage_sections: 32 bit big, static version: pspy32 download
64 bit big, static version: pspy64 download
p: enables printing commands to stdout (enabled by default)

arguments.name: --debug
arguments.description: prints verbose error messages which are otherwise hidden.

examples: 2018/02/18 21:00:03 Inotify watchers set up: Watching 1030 directories - watching now
documentation.how_to_use: Install/setup: docker run -it --rm local/pspy-example:latest. Run the primary entrypoint as shown in upstream docs, e.g. `./pspy64 -pf -i 1000`. Further invocations from upstream: `./pspy64 -r /path/to/first/recursive/dir -r /path/to/second/recursive/dir -d /path/to/the/non-recursive/dir`; `./pspy64 -p=false -f`. pspy - unprivileged Linux process snooping.
documentation.when_to_use: Use during the 'any' phase against surfaces [shell_linux] in the Penetration Testing category. Fits when the engagement needs: pspy - unprivileged Linux process snooping.
documentation.why_to_use: Prefer this tool based on upstream description: pspy - unprivileged Linux process snooping.

trust.warning: Attribution/index entry only. Audit provenance, code, releases and licence before use.

use_cases: When you need Penetration Testing, use pspy because pspy - unprivileged Linux process snooping.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `./pspy64 -pf -i 1000` as documented upstream for pspy.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `./pspy64 -r /path/to/first/recursive/dir -r /path/to/second/recursive/dir -d /path/to/the/non-recursive/dir` as documented upstream for pspy.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `./pspy64 -p=false -f` as documented upstream for pspy.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `docker run -it --rm local/pspy-example:latest` as documented upstream for pspy.
When chaining Penetration Testing tools, pair pspy with sibling catalog entries sharing its attack_surface/phase_hint.
When validating lab setup, run `pspy --help` (or the README entrypoint) and confirm required hardware/deps from the catalog.
